title: Partition of India and Pakistan
summary: At midnight on 14-15 August 1947 the British Raj ended and two successor dominions came into being: Pakistan on 14 August and India on 15 August. The partition line drawn by a British barrister who had never been to India cut through the Punjab and Bengal and displaced an estimated 15 million people in the largest migration in modern human history. Somewhere between several hundred thousand and two million people died in the accompanying communal violence. The two new states, born fighting over Kashmir, would remain adversaries for the rest of the century and beyond.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The partition was the endpoint of British rule that had begun with the East India Company in the seventeenth century, had become direct Crown government after the uprising of 1857, and had for eighty years managed a subcontinent of extraordinary religious, linguistic, and social diversity through a mixture of coercion and cultivated collaboration. The rise of the Indian National Congress after 1885 as a broadly non-sectarian nationalist body, and of the All-India Muslim League after 1906 to represent specifically Muslim interests, produced two distinct if overlapping political projects. Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi's mass campaigns of non-cooperation (1920-22), civil disobedience (1930-34), and Quit India (1942) had made independence a mass demand.

The communal question sharpened in the 1930s and 1940s. The Government of India Act of 1935 introduced provincial autonomy, and the 1937 elections produced Congress ministries in most provinces but poor Muslim League results. Muhammad Ali Jinnah, the London-trained Karachi lawyer who had once been called the ambassador of Hindu-Muslim unity, drew from that experience the conclusion that Muslims needed constitutional guarantees the Congress would not concede. The Lahore Resolution of 23 March 1940 called for autonomous, sovereign Muslim-majority units in the north-west and east of India; over the next years the demand crystallised as Pakistan. The Second World War, into which the viceroy took India without consulting Congress in September 1939, drove Congress out of the ministries and gave the League room to consolidate. By the 1945-46 elections it had swept the reserved Muslim seats.

The postwar Labour government of Clement Attlee, presiding over a bankrupt Britain and a war-weary electorate, was determined to leave. The Cabinet Mission of March-June 1946 tried to preserve a united India inside a loose three-tier federation; both Congress and the League at first accepted the plan and then quarrelled over its interpretation. Jinnah called for Direct Action on 16 August 1946 to press the demand for Pakistan; in Calcutta the day produced four days of massacre in which several thousand Hindus and Muslims died. Communal killings spread to Noakhali in East Bengal, Bihar, and, in early 1947, the Punjab. In February 1947 Attlee announced that the British would leave India no later than June 1948 and sent Lord Louis Mountbatten as the last viceroy. Mountbatten's own reading of the situation, reinforced by mounting violence, led him to bring the date forward to August 1947 and to propose partition into two dominions rather than any looser confederation. Nehru and Vallabhbhai Patel of Congress and Jinnah of the League accepted the 3 June plan.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The partition machinery was designed and executed at extraordinary speed. On 15 July 1947 the British Parliament passed the Indian Independence Act, which gave force to the 3 June plan and set 15 August 1947 as the date of transfer of power. Two boundary commissions, one each for the Punjab and Bengal, were appointed under the chairmanship of Cyril Radcliffe, a British barrister who had never visited India, with Muslim and non-Muslim assessors nominated by the League and the Congress. Radcliffe was given about five weeks and used outdated census data and small-scale maps. His awards, kept sealed until 17 August so as not to complicate the independence celebrations, split the Punjab and Bengal along lines that assigned Muslim-majority areas to Pakistan and non-Muslim-majority areas to India, and produced Pakistan in two wings, west and east, separated by more than 1,600 kilometres of Indian territory. The princely states, some 565 in number, were told to accede to one or other dominion; almost all did.

On the night of 14-15 August the constituent assemblies of the two dominions took the transfer of power. In Karachi, Jinnah was sworn in as governor-general of Pakistan on 14 August. In New Delhi at midnight Jawaharlal Nehru delivered his 'Tryst with Destiny' speech: 'Long years ago we made a tryst with destiny, and now the time has come when we shall redeem our pledge...'. Gandhi, absent from the celebrations, spent the night in Calcutta on a hunger strike to check the communal killings. Mountbatten stayed on as first governor-general of India.

The human catastrophe that followed had few historical parallels. In the Punjab, whose 33 million people were roughly half Muslim and half Sikh and Hindu, columns of refugees, kafilas of tens of thousands on foot, in bullock carts, or crammed onto trains, moved in opposite directions across the new border. Trains were stopped and slaughtered; villages were attacked; women were abducted in the tens of thousands; some killed themselves or were killed by kinsmen to prevent conversion. In Bengal the violence was less concentrated but still terrible. Serious modern estimates put the number of dead at between 200,000 and two million; the number of displaced people at fifteen million; the number of women abducted at around 100,000. Local princes ordered massacres of minority populations; whole neighbourhoods burned in Lahore, Amritsar, and Delhi. The old imperial administration collapsed; the new dominion governments improvised.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The partition set the pattern of South Asian politics for generations. Kashmir, whose Hindu maharaja Hari Singh had not acceded to either dominion by 15 August, was invaded by Pakistan-backed tribal irregulars from the North-West Frontier in October 1947; on 26 October he acceded to India and Indian troops were airlifted to Srinagar. The first India-Pakistan war ended in a UN-brokered ceasefire in January 1949 that left roughly a third of the state under Pakistani control and set the disputed Line of Control that endures today. Kashmir has produced three further wars (1965, 1971, and Kargil in 1999), a long armed insurgency from the late 1980s, and a permanent standoff between two nuclear-armed neighbours since 1998.

Gandhi's response to partition was despair and defiance. He fasted, walked, and reconciled Muslims and Hindus in Bengal and Delhi through the winter of 1947-48. On 30 January 1948 he was shot and killed at Birla House in New Delhi by Nathuram Godse, a Hindu nationalist who blamed him for being too indulgent to Pakistan. Jinnah, ill with tuberculosis for years, died in Karachi on 11 September 1948, only a year after Pakistan's founding, leaving the young country to work out its institutions without him. The Indian constitution promulgated on 26 January 1950 made India a secular parliamentary republic with universal adult franchise; Pakistan struggled through drafts and coups before promulgating its first constitution in 1956.

Economic consequences were immediate and durable. Bengal's jute industry, whose fields were in East Pakistan and whose mills were around Calcutta, was cut in two; Punjab's integrated canal system was fought over until the Indus Waters Treaty of 1960. The refugee flows continued for years, and further catastrophic movements followed the war of 1971 that split East Pakistan from the rest and created independent Bangladesh, itself accompanied by mass killings and the flight of some ten million people to India. Successive rounds of communal violence, from Ahmedabad in 1969 to Delhi in 1984, Mumbai in 1992-93, Gujarat in 2002, and Delhi in 2020, have all been read, in part, through the memory of 1947.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The two dominions that took power in August 1947 have travelled markedly different roads. India, despite recurrent tests, has retained a competitive parliamentary system, held regular elections, and grown into the world's most populous democracy and one of its largest economies. Pakistan has alternated between civilian and military rule and endured the loss of its eastern wing in 1971 as the independent state of Bangladesh. Both states are nuclear powers and their disputes over Kashmir, water, and militant groups remain unresolved. Roughly 3.4 million Indians and Pakistanis now live in each other's countries or once did, and family stories of 1947, of trains, of ancestral houses left behind, of neighbours turned killers or saviours, remain central to the region's cultural memory.

The partition has also had a large diasporic afterlife. Migrations to Britain, Canada, the United States, the Gulf, and elsewhere carried the arguments of 1947 across the world. The Partition Museum in Amritsar, opened in 2017, and the 1947 Partition Archive, based in Berkeley, California, have collected tens of thousands of oral histories from survivors, most of them now in old age. Novelists from Saadat Hasan Manto and Bhisham Sahni to Salman Rushdie, filmmakers from Deepa Mehta to Ritwik Ghatak, and historians such as Ayesha Jalal, Gyanendra Pandey, and Yasmin Khan have made partition one of the most studied catastrophes of the twentieth century.

Internationally the partition set precedents whose echoes are audible in Palestine (1947-48), Cyprus (1974), Yugoslavia (1990s), and Sudan (2011). Its record as a solution to communal conflict is at best mixed. It is now widely regarded as a case study in what can go wrong when imperial powers, in retreat, hand political power over quickly, on lines drawn hastily, to leaders whose electorates have already been mobilised on religious identity. What is not in doubt is its scale as a hinge event. On 14-15 August 1947 the world's largest empire ended, the world's largest democracy began, and the world's largest short-term migration was set in motion, all within a single set of ceremonies.

worldContext.text: China in August 1947 was in the middle of its civil war. Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist forces had launched a major offensive against Communist-held Yan'an in March, but Mao Zedong's People's Liberation Army was recovering and Liu Bocheng's forces crossed the Yellow River in a decisive summer counter-offensive. Occupied Japan, under the Supreme Command of General Douglas MacArthur, had adopted a new constitution in May 1947 renouncing war and setting up a parliamentary democracy. In Korea, US and Soviet occupation forces continued to divide the peninsula at the 38th parallel.

August 1947 in the Middle East was dominated by the coming partition of another British mandate. The UN Special Committee on Palestine (UNSCOP), which had toured the country in June and July, reported on 31 August recommending partition into a Jewish and an Arab state, a plan the UN General Assembly would adopt on 29 November 1947. In Egypt, King Farouk's government referred the dispute over British troops in the Suez Canal Zone to the UN Security Council. In Iran, Prime Minister Ahmad Qavam's fragile coalition was falling apart over Soviet oil concessions.

Africa in 1947 was still overwhelmingly colonial, but nationalist parties were consolidating. In the Gold Coast, Kwame Nkrumah returned from Britain in December 1947 as general secretary of the United Gold Coast Convention, the party from which he would break away to form the Convention People's Party. In French West Africa, the Rassemblement Democratique Africain led by Felix Houphouet-Boigny was pressing for reform. In Southern Rhodesia and Kenya, wartime service and postwar economic strains fed African political mobilisation. In South Africa, the National Party was preparing the campaign that would bring apartheid to power in 1948.

Europe in August 1947 was living through the coldest peace of the postwar. The Marshall Plan, announced by US Secretary of State George Marshall at Harvard on 5 June 1947, was being negotiated in Paris; the Soviet Union refused to participate and forbade eastern European states to do so, sharpening the emerging Cold War division. Britain, exhausted and near bankrupt, had already told Washington in February that it could no longer support Greece and Turkey, prompting the Truman Doctrine. Communist parties would seize full power in Czechoslovakia in February 1948.

The United States in August 1947 was reorganising for a Cold War world. The National Security Act, signed by President Harry Truman on 26 July, created the Central Intelligence Agency, the National Security Council, and unified the armed forces under a new Department of Defense. The Rio Treaty of Reciprocal Assistance was signed at Petropolis, Brazil, on 2 September, binding the American republics to hemispheric defence. In Latin America, Juan Peron's Argentine government was consolidating; Chile's Radical government had just outlawed the Communist Party.

Australia and New Zealand in 1947 were beginning the postwar migration schemes that would remake their societies over the coming decade. Australia had received the first shipments of European Displaced Persons in November 1947 under a plan negotiated by Immigration Minister Arthur Calwell. Both dominions supported early recognition of India and Pakistan and would host students under the Colombo Plan from 1950. In the Pacific, the UN Trusteeship Council formalised the transition of former Japanese-mandated islands to US strategic trusteeship.

figures.name: Mohandas K. Gandhi
figures.role: Leader of the Indian independence movement, opponent of partition, assassinated in January 1948

figures.name: Jawaharlal Nehru
figures.role: First prime minister of independent India, delivered the 'Tryst with Destiny' speech at midnight on 14-15 August 1947

figures.name: Muhammad Ali Jinnah
figures.role: Leader of the All-India Muslim League and first governor-general of Pakistan, known as Quaid-e-Azam

figures.name: Vallabhbhai Patel
figures.role: Congress leader and first deputy prime minister of India, principal architect of the integration of the princely states

figures.name: Lord Louis Mountbatten
figures.role: Last viceroy of British India and first governor-general of the Dominion of India

figures.name: Cyril Radcliffe
figures.role: British barrister who chaired the Punjab and Bengal boundary commissions and drew the border between India and Pakistan

images.caption: Jawaharlal Nehru signing the Indian constitution; Nehru delivered the 'Tryst with Destiny' speech at the transfer of power on 14-15 August 1947.

images.caption: Muhammad Ali Jinnah, founder of Pakistan, photographed around 1945.
A Sikh family on the move during the partition of Punjab in 1947.

sources.title: The Great Divide: The violent legacy of Indian partition
